reviews.review_msg: Super disorganized and the lectures are sometimes 100+ slides with no particular order. She just randomly throws theories to you without connecting them or explaining their significance. Easy class, horrible teacher. Finished with an A but still learned nothing.

Initially gave Meg the benefit of the doubt, but found reviews were very accurate. She rushes through 150+ slides or goes off-topic, making lectures confusing. She and TAs rarely respond on Canvas, and slides are ALWAYS uploaded late. Even her study guide didn't help—MC questions were oddly worded. Avoid this class unless the structure improves.

Overall, she is a very decent instructor and is passionate about the materials she teaches. However, she did miss a lot of classes due to her sick days. But when she is available, she did a great job introducing many concepts that turned out to be useful for AI research.

I really enjoyed COGS100 with Margaret Grant. Yes, there is a lot of course content to cover and there are a few readings each week, but if you engage with the material it is straightforward and enjoyable. I liked this format of hybrid teaching. Lectures were great. Assignments and tests not too difficult. Highly recommend taking a course with her.

her lectures were confusing, with her slides being full of words but no clear indication to what we were learning. this was a hybrid class, so we we were required to do 4/5 participation assignments plus "an hour" of extra reading outside of lectures. she focused on linguistics more than cognitive science. seems nice, but not the best prof.

Awful lecturer who's main focus was on the linguistics side of COGS and hardly anything else. Gave out way too many readings to fill in the 1 hour of designated online time for the course. Made 0 effort to prepare her students for any exams and never responded to emails/canvas messages. Took as an elective yet is somehow my lowest grade. Avoid her.

Way too much work for a base 100 level class. 70+ slides every plus readings plus extra videos to watch every single week. Midterms and final are extremely tough, asking very specific questions, including who says... and minute details that come from her 1000+ slides. Not a good class for an introduction to cognitive science nor for GPA boost.

The lectures were dry and she covered a lot of material very quickly but then in the exams, the questions were more detailed. There were two midterms and one cumulative final. The course didn't feel like a 100-level course due to tough exams.

The problem was that Margaret specializes in linguistics, so the content tended to be more about the linguistic side of cognitive science. I'm not sure how much she really knew about the other topics in cognitive science but I was still able to learn a lot. She also seemed a bit unorganized at times but she was caring towards the students.
there was a LOT of content covered in this class. but she explained and also had lecture recordings. make sure you prepare your written questions well for the exam. good luck

Meg Grant is an awful professor. She's a nice person, but she should not be teaching this course. She has a linguistics background and made half the course about that. Lectures are extremely boring, and exams are memory based. Grading criteria for papers and exam questions is unclear and inconsistent. I regret taking this class.

I don't think this course is as bad as some reviews are making it out to be. It's definitely a tougher 100 level class so some prior knowledge in phil and psyc helps a lot. Study the main ideas (section summaries) in the slides for the exams, don't try to memorize every detail as the slides can be long.

Decent prof who cares about the topic and students a lot. Course content is not for most first-year students. Without a foundation to build on, I can see how the topics can be overwhelming, no matter how detailed the lectures or how easy the tests are. I would advise taking intro psyc courses beforehand at the very least.

Im sorry but she is not a good teacher. She tends to ramble on and on about stuff that isn't that important and barley goes over the important topics. I spend hours and hours just trying to understand one lecture and her lectures are so info-heavy. However for the midterms she has given us the written questions prior so we can prepare for those.

DO NOT TAKE THIS CLASS AS AN ELECTIVE! She makes this class so info-heavy, some of her lectures are 70+ slides. Some topics are very complex and she is not good at explaining. She gives like two long readings each week and sometimes videos too. Overall I would say this class is just too info heavy with 12 units and some have more than 1 topic.

Utter confusion for a 100 level class. I spent more time looking up word definitions than actual reading. I takes me ~4hrs to listen to the lecture and make enough sense to be able to make notes. I haven't got a grade lower than B-, and not sure I will pass this class. Lectures are highjacked by rambling off topic questions, so very frustrating

Lots of very dense cogs readings that are hard to understand. This course was a tough one to get through. But it was fair with clear marking requirements. There was a group project. I got an amazing group however (your mileage may vary on that part). She was always very quick to respond to emails and answer any questions as well.

In the group project, two of us did most of the work. We lost a lot of points because of this. This was very unfair to those of us who put in the work. I wish the textbook was used, it would have made studying easier.

If you catch a marking mistake, she won't even bother replying to your email. This has been my least engaging course at SFU of my three years. She records all lectures, but she bothered posting for weeks 1, 2, and 8. Which is quite deceiving because that's the drop deadline. I wish I waited for another prof to teach this course.

At first I found her and the course harder than expected for a first year course but she's incredibly thorough in the material and once I understood her teaching style I found her to be very open and fair. Not the most engaging orator but clearly knows her stuff. I appreciate that she tried very hard to adapt for online and tries to engage 1on1.

One of the most well-organized remote classes that I've taken! Her lectures are clear and straightforward rather than 2-3 hours of fluff. It felt like Meg tried really hard to lessen the stress on us during this time and I really enjoyed this class! It does take a lot of work to do well, but textbook is free!

Meg is a solid prof! Her lectures are quite good, and I love the textbook she uses for this course - it is so helpful! This class definitely needs a lot of practice and application of the things you learn in class in order to get a good mark, but if you practice, it is very doable. Her assignments are helpful for practice on the test.

Meg is a good professor. The online textbook that she uses (for free) is very well written, and serves as an excellent resource. Her lectures are great some weeks, and more confusing on others. She gives multiple assignments and tests with no midterm/final so the grades are a fairly balanced representation. This course gets progressively harder

I don't get why she gets a bad rating for other classes but from my personal experience, she runs one of the most efficiently structured online classes, despite the challenging times. She returns homework feedback before tests so it really helps to learn from mistakes. Her lectures are concise and clear and help with hw a lot. Tests are very fair

Margaret isn't a good teacher, even though she boasts of all her experience. She teaches mainly theory, not application, and then doesn't understand why people can't do the work. Her tests are very difficult and a little bit outrageous at times. She can be very passive aggressive as well. Beware.

Im not sure about other courses, for cogs 100 she teaches as if you know stuff prior, if you don't have a background in philosophy, bio, comp sci etc, you are not going to understand a single word in class. She treats the class as if its a upper-level course

COGS 200 with Prof Grant is fun and fairly involved. She covers a lot of material in the course with plenty of mandatory readings that can be quite time consuming. For a second-year level course it is not difficult but does demand a lot of time to be successful in. Prof. Grant is a solid lecturer and cares about the students.

Her exams are laughably easy, and assignments are quite easy as well. The course is interesting, but she is so slow and that kills all the anticipation. Ended up skipping some of her lectures because of the slow pacing, but it's not a problem - everything's up on Canvas and do attend the tutorials! Otherwise she is fine, no complaints.
Class is too time-consuming and stressful

So apparently this is Meg's first term at SFU from Germany. She is really nice that's for sure, but her lectures are so long (3hr class 80 ppt slides roughly). She has easy online assignments, but 2 hard essays that are hard to get a good grade (class average was 13-15 out of 20). The midterm was tricky like 50% roughly but she curved it thankfully
spent most of my time during lecture counting her usage of the word 'um'.. horrible course.. marking on the paper makes it hard to get a high grade. do not take thinking it is a gpa booster

Margaret is a kind professor; however, her grading scale and expectations are ridiculous for a first year level class, as well as the class averages. Class average for an essay paper was mid-60s, with the highest being mid-80s. If you take her class, be aware that your gpa may go down
